---
title: "Diagrams"
page-layout: article
format:
  html:
    include-before-body: _lfl-nav.html
---
### General-purpose packages for drawing diagrams

* **[pgf and TikZ](https://ctan.org/pkg/pgf)**: The modern standard for generating vector graphics natively in LaTeX. It uses an intuitive syntax layer (TikZ) and is capable of drawing everything from simple graphs to complex geometric figures.
* **[XY-pic](http://www.tug.org/applications/Xy-pic/)**: A powerful legacy alternative. While exceptionally versatile, its syntax is famously dense and cryptic compared to TikZ. There is an extensive chapter detailing its use in *The LaTeX Graphics Companion*.

### Diagrams for modal logicians (Kripke frames etc.)

* **[A Quick TikZ Guide for Modal Logicians](http://www.actual.world/resources/tex/doc/TikZ.pdf)** (Alex Kocurek): A tutorial on how to use TikZ specifically for drawing Kripke frames, accessibility relations, etc.
* **[Diagrams of sphere models for variably strict conditionals](https://github.com/rzach/ptolemaic-astronomy)** (Richard Zach): Offers TikZ templates for drawing Lewis-style sphere semantics.

### Commutative diagrams for category theory

For an extensive historical overview, see J.S. Milne's [Guide to commutative diagrams packages](http://www.jmilne.org/not/CDGuide.pdf). However, the undisputed modern standard is:

* **[tikz-cd](http://www.ctan.org/pkg/tikz-cd)**: This package simplifies the creation of commutative diagrams by layering a convenient syntax over TikZ. It delivers beautiful default spacing and arrow styles out of the box.

Because TikZ must calculate complex vector paths, diagram-heavy documents can slow down compilation significantly. To fix this, try pairing it with the **[memoize](https://ctan.org/pkg/memoize)** package. `memoize` automatically caches compiled graphics, meaning unchanged diagrams aren't re-processed every time you typeset, speeding up your workflow.

* **[diagrams.tex](http://www.paultaylor.eu/diagrams/)** (Paul Taylor): A classic macro package for drawing commutative diagrams. While highly rated in the past, it has been almost entirely superseded by `tikz-cd` in modern LaTeX workflows.




*Updated 4 June 2026*
